question: What is the key property of a max-heap?
options: The root node contains the largest key.
The root node contains the smallest key.
It is a complete binary tree where all levels are full.
It is a balanced binary search tree.
explanation: The defining property of a max-heap is that the key of any node is greater than or equal to the keys of its children, meaning the largest element is always at the root.

question: What is the worst-case time complexity of the Heapsort algorithm?
options: O(n log n)
O(n²)
O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Heapsort has a guaranteed worst-case time complexity of O(n log n) because building the heap takes O(n) time and each of the n-1 extractions takes O(log n) time.

question: In the context of the heapify operation, what is the time complexity of maintaining the heap property for a subtree rooted at a given node?
options: O(h), where h is the height of the subtree
O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the heap
O(log n), where n is the number of nodes in the heap
explanation: The time complexity for heapify on a single node is proportional to the height of the subtree it needs to traverse, which is O(h). In a heap, the height h is O(log n).

question: Given an array representation of a heap, what is the index of the left child of the node at index i?
options: 2i
explanation: In a zero-indexed array representation of a heap, the left child of the node at index i is located at index 2i + 1. The right child is at 2i + 2, and the parent is at floor((i-1)/2).

question: Why is the overall time complexity of the BUILD-MAX-HEAP operation only O(n) and not O(n log n), even though it calls MAX-HEAPIFY (O(log n)) on many nodes?
options: Because most of the calls to MAX-HEAPIFY are on nodes near the leaves, which have small heights.
Because it uses a bottom-up approach that reduces the average cost.
Because the loop runs only for n/2 nodes.
Both A and B are correct.
explanation: The O(n) complexity is achieved through a bottom-up approach that starts from the last non-leaf node. While it runs on n/2 nodes, the key is that most nodes are near the bottom of the tree where the cost of heapify is low. A precise summation of the costs proves the linear bound.
